title: Biochemistry, Apolipoprotein B -- Clinical Significance
content: A promising new area in lipid-lowering therapies is that of antisense oligonucleotides to target mRNA of proteins that are involved in cholesterol metabolism. Initial trials of apoB antisense oligos have shown promise with a 50% reduction in Apo B levels, a 30% reduction in LDL levels, and decreased cardiovascular risk.  However, they cause liver dysfunction. A new goal of the 2018 NCEP guidelines is the inclusion of apoB, stating that an apoB greater than 130 mg/dL is a risk-enhancing factor and requires measurement in primary prevention treatment protocols, and this is an essential step towards the analysis of apoB for cardiovascular risk. [5]

title: Mipomersen(Archived) -- Indications
content: Mipomersen is a second-generation novel agent identified as an antisense oligonucleotide inhibitor of apolipoprotein B-100 (apoB-100). It is indicated in the management of homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia.

title: Mipomersen(Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
content: By directly reducing and eliminating the cytoplasmic apolipoprotein B-100 messenger RNA concentration, mipomersen plays a crucial role in lowering the ribosomal production of the final, folded apolipoprotein B-100 polypeptide. This therapy ultimately leads to decreased production of protein human apolipoprotein B-100 and, therefore, reduces the production of low-density lipoprotein and lower-low-density lipoprotein cholesterol. Mipomersen functioned consistently across all species in tested human volunteers and patients.

title: Mipomersen(Archived) -- Indications -- FDA Approved Use
content: The U.S. Food and Drug Administration approves Mipomersen as an adjunct to current lipid-lowering therapy and diet modifications to limit low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C), apolipoprotein B-100 (apoB), and non-high-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol in patients diagnosed with homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (HoFH). [1] [2] A combination of therapies may be utilized for patients unable to achieve their target low-density lipoprotein-cholesterol levels.

title: Lipoprotein Lipase Deficiency -- Treatment / Management -- Novel Treatments
content: Fibrates can raise HDL and lower triglycerides by stimulating peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor-alpha (PPARα). A novel selective PPARα modulator, pemafibrate, effectively lowers triglycerides and apoC-III. Unlike older fibrates, which have limited PPARα activation due to their nonselectivity, pemafibrate is 2500 times more potent than fenofibrate in activating PPARα activation. [47] The large-scale PROMINENT trial demonstrated significant reductions in triglycerides, VLDL, remnant cholesterol, and apoC-III, although it did not improve cardiac outcomes, which was the trial's primary end point. [47] [52] [53]

title: Lomitapide -- Continuing Education Activity
content: Lomitapide could be beneficial for HoFH patients exhibiting insufficient response to PCSK9 inhibitor treatment, those with atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and a baseline LDL-C of 190 mg/dL or higher, and those who demonstrate insufficient responses to statins, whether used alone or in combination with PCSK9 inhibitors or ezetimibe. Lomitapide rapidly binds to and inhibits microsomal triglyceride transfer protein (MTP) within the endoplasmic reticulum lumen, which impedes the synthesis of lipoproteins containing apo-B in both hepatocytes and enterocytes. This process reduces the production of very low-density lipoprotein and chylomicrons, thereby lowering plasma LDL-C levels. The safety and efficacy of lomitapide have not been established for heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia. This activity elucidates the indications, mechanism of action, pharmacokinetics, adverse event profile, and other relevant interactions essential for interprofessional healthcare team members in treating patients with HoFH and related conditions.

title: Lomitapide -- Continuing Education Activity
content: Lomitapide and mipomersen are antihyperlipidemic agents indicated for treating homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (HoFH). Lomitapide is approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for use alongside other antilipidemic drugs and a low-fat diet to lower serum lipids, decrease low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol (LDL-C), total cholesterol, apolipoprotein B (apo-B), and non–high-density lipoprotein cholesterol in patients with HoFH. Patients with HoFH might present with arcus corneae, tendinous xanthomas, xanthelasma, and planar or tuberous xanthomas.

title: Familial Hypercholesterolemia -- Treatment / Management -- Drug Therapies
content: Statins are the standard therapy for familial hypercholesterolemia. All guidelines recommend statins as first-line drugs for patients with FH, with a goal of reaching maximally tolerated doses. [33] The effect of statins has been well studied, and most randomized clinical trials observed a reduction of around 50 percent of the initial untreated LDL levels, as well as a reduction in cardiovascular events. [34] [35] [36] Unfortunately, a large number of patients with FH, even on maximally-tolerated statins alone, either do not show this level of reduction in LDL levels, or they do not reach their goal LDL levels, even with a 50 percent reduction, because their initial LDL levels are so high. [35] Second-line therapies include ezetimibe and PCSK9 inhibitors. Ezetimibe has been showing to add an extra 10 to 30 percent reduction in LDL levels for patients on maximally tolerated statins. [37] [38] Currently, ezetimibe is the drug of choice as the second line in most guidelines. [39] PCSK9 inhibitors have shown a great reduction in LDL levels and cardiovascular outcomes in different randomized clinical trials. [22] [40] In those trials, PCKS9 inhibitors lowered LDL levels by 50 to 60 percent. While exhibiting higher efficacy than ezetimibe, the use of PCSK9 inhibitors is limited by their high cost and the reluctance of insurance companies to approve their use. [41] Hence, PCSK9 inhibitors can be used as second or third-line drugs for patients with familial hypercholesterolemia. Fourth line therapies include mipomersen (mRNA inhibition of apolipoprotein B), lomitapide (microsomal triglyceride transfer protein inhibition), niacin, lipoprotein apheresis, ileal bypass surgery, and liver transplantation. [42] [43] [44] [45] These are usually reserved if the LDL does not reach the target level using statins, ezetimibe, and PCSK9 inhibitors. [44]

title: Familial Hypertriglyceridemia -- Treatment / Management -- Statins
content: Olezarsen, administered as a monthly injection, has recently received a "fast-track" designation from the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for treating familial chylomicronemia and has shown promise in managing FHTG. It is a synthetically modified DNA fragment that targets hepatocyte mRNA to inhibit apolipoprotein C-III (APOC3) synthesis, thus enhancing the clearance of triglyceride-rich lipoproteins. Clinical trials have demonstrated a reduction in plasma triglyceride levels by approximately 60% after 6 months. [37] [38] [39] [40]

title: Abetalipoproteinemia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Evidence-based Medicine
content: Because of the rarity of the disorder, there is a lack of randomized clinical trials on the long-term benefits of vitamins. However, small case series and anecdotal reports indicate that when patients are diagnosed and referred early to a clinic that specializes in the management of this lipid disorder, the outcomes are improved. [10] There is ample evidence indicating that prompt supplementation with vitamins can improve the prognosis and outcome. [11] [Level 3] However, the long-term outlook for many of these patients is guarded as many go on to develop vision loss and permanent blindness.

title: Hypercholesterolemia -- Treatment / Management
content: Treatment of heterozygotes with HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors may normalize LDL levels. However, achieving optimal levels may require one of the combinations involving reductase inhibitors, niacin, bile acid sequestrants, and ezetimibe. Levels of LDL cholesterol less than 100 mg/dL can be obtained with combinations of these drugs in some patients. Treatment of individuals with homozygosity or combined heterozygosity is challenging. Partial control may be achieved with medications including antisense oligonucleotide directed at Apo B-100 synthesis, inhibition of microsomal triglyceride transfer protein, and ezetimibe. Statins and monoclonal antibodies directed at proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9 (PCSK9) protein are useful if some residual receptor activity is present and there is no null mutation. LDL apheresis in conjunction with medications can be very effective. Striking reduction of LDL levels is observed after liver transplantation, illustrating the important role of hepatic receptors in LDL metabolism.

Innovative targeting strategies have enhanced the efficacy of these novel interventions while substantially improving their tolerability. [38] [39]

content: Apolipoprotein A acts as a surrogate marker of HDL plasma concentration, and evidence of the utility of the ApoB: Apo A ratio as a predictor of cardiovascular disease (CVD) has been reported, especially in the INTERHEART study. However, given the controversy about the role of HDL in preventing atherosclerosis, there is no strong indication to measure apoA.

title: PCSK9 Inhibitors -- Continuing Education Activity
content: Proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9 (PCSK9) has emerged as an important regulator of cholesterol metabolism. In the United States, 2 fully humanized monoclonal antibodies—alirocumab and evolocumab— have been approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to inhibit or reduce PCSK9 activity. Recently, both the European Union and FDA have approved inclisiran, a small interfering mRNA, that inhibits intracellular PCSK9 synthesis. Increased PCSK9 activity correlates with heightened LDL-C levels, while specific gain-of-function mutations lead to autosomal dominant familial hypercholesterolemia characterized by extremely high total and low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels, premature atherosclerotic vascular disease, and the formation of tendon xanthomas. In most cases, this is due to a genetic mutation of the LDL receptor caused by mutations of the apoprotein B100 or PCSK9 genes. Conversely, individuals with reduced PCSK9 activity, whether due to genetic polymorphism or the administration of PCSK9 monoclonal antibodies, exhibit lower cholesterol levels and a reduced risk of cardiovascular disease. This activity outlines the indications, mechanism of action, administration methods, significant adverse effects, contraindications, monitoring, and toxicity of PCSK9 inhibitors. This activity also helps the interprofessional healthcare team tailor patient therapy for optimal outcomes in serum lipid management and improve cardiovascular outcomes.

title: Mipomersen(Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
content: Mipomersen is a 20-base pair, 2’-O-methoxyethyl modified antisense oligonucleotide complementary to human apolipoprotein B-100 messenger RNA. [3] Mipomersen aims to identify and hybridize with the human apolipoprotein B-100 mRNA via the traditional deoxyribonucleotide or ribonucleotide base pairing mechanism. The interface between the complementary mipomersen therapeutic oligonucleotide with the newly synthesized human apolipoprotein B-100 leads to the degradation of the messenger RNA. Most frequently, the hybridization of mipomersen to its target mRNA leads to the recruitment of RNase H1, a catalytic enzyme that degrades ribonucleic acid in the cellular cytoplasm. [4]

title: Lomitapide -- Indications
content: Lomitapide is an antihyperlipidemic agent indicated for treating homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (HoFH). Lomitapide is approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for use alongside other antihyperlipidemic agents along with a low-fat diet to reduce serum lipids, involving LDL apheresis, to reduce low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol (LDL-C), total cholesterol, apolipoprotein B (apo-B), and non-high-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol (non–HDL-C) in patients with HoFH. [1] [2] Patients with HoFH might present with arcus corneae, tendinous xanthomas, xanthelasma, and planar or tuberous xanthomas. Planar xanthomas are flat or somewhat elevated yellowish patches. Tuberous xanthomas are painless, hard nodules usually on the extensor joints’ surfaces. The safety and efficacy of lomitapide are not established for heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia.

title: Biochemistry, Apolipoprotein B -- Pathophysiology
content: Familial defective apo B is an autosomal dominant disorder that arises due to a mutation in apo B that prevents binding of the defective apo B to the LDL receptor. This condition results in a clinical phenotype similar to classical familial hypercholesterolemia due to mutations in the LDL receptor with increased LDL-cholesterol, xanthomas, and premature ASCVD, underscoring the importance of apoB in atherogenesis. The most frequent variant is apoB-3500, caused by a point mutation resulting in a substitution of glutamine for arginine at position 3500. [1] This mutation is an excellent illustration of a defect in an important ligand resulting in premature ASCVD.

title: Dysbetalipoproteinemia -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological Therapy
content: There are limited randomized controlled trials specific for familial dysbetalipoproteinemia patients evaluating the effect of lipid-lowering medications. The completed trials did have indeterminate endpoints in terms of on-target lipid levels. Overall trials did demonstrate a reduction in LDL and total cholesterol levels. [36] [37] [38] [39] The role of PCSK9 inhibitors remains unclear in FD. Some studies report that the dominant portion of apoB-containing lipoproteins is decreased with PCSK9 inhibition, such as LDL in mixed hyperlipidemia and hypercholesterolemia, and cholesterol-rich VLDL, remnants, and LDL in familial dysbetalipoproteinemia. PCSK9 inhibitors could be helpful in patients who are resistant or intolerant to statin or fibrate therapy. [40]

title: Low HDL Cholesterol -- Treatment / Management
content: The infusion of apolipoprotein A-I (apoA-I) has been investigated for its potential cardiovascular benefits, as apoA-I levels are strongly associated with a reduced risk of cardiovascular disease in patients already on statin therapy. [24] A large study examined the effects of consecutive weekly infusions of apoA-I versus placebo after a recent myocardial infarction to determine the benefits of apoA-I infusion therapy. [25] Patients who received the apoA-I infusion had an increase in cholesterol efflux capacity. However, further research is needed to determine if this therapy will translate to a reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events.

content: HoFH is less responsive to drug therapy than HeFH. Thus LDL-C apheresis (LA) has been developed as a primary management strategy. Additional options for patients with HoFH include the microsomal triglyceride transfer protein (MTP) inhibitor, lomitapide, and the apoB antisense oligonucleotide (ASO), mipomersen, especially when LA is not available. title: Pitavastatin -- Indications
content: Pitavastatin is currently FDA approved for the management of primary dyslipidemia and mixed dyslipidemia as adjunctive therapy to dietary changes to help lower total cholesterol, low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL), apolipoprotein B (Apo B), triglycerides (TG), and to increase high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL). [1] [2] [3] It is also FDA approved to treat heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (HeFH) in pediatric patients over 8 years of age. [4] [5] Unlike other statins, pitavastatin has not been studied in any large randomized controlled study to determine whether its use is associated with decreased cardiovascular events.

title: Evinacumab -- Indications
content: The diagnosis of HoFH is clinical; genetic testing should be performed if readily available. Possessing two mutant alleles for LDLR , Apo[b] , PCSK9 , or LDLR adaptor protein gene locus 1 is considered a positive test for HoFH. Clinically, an LDL-C ≥400 mg/dL and one or both parents having clinically diagnosed FH, positive genetic testing for a known LDL-C–raising ( LDLR , PCSK9 , Apo[b] ) gene defect, or autosomal-recessive FH indicates a patient has HoFH. An LDL-C >560 mg/dL or LDL-C >400 mg/dL with either aortic valve disease or xanthomas at 20 years of age or younger could also meet the HoFH diagnosis criteria.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2538
content: Inclisiran and evolocumab are both classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “Other lipid modifying agents.” This classification indicates that each drug is used to modify lipid concentrations, particularly low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol, and is employed in the treatment of hyperlipidaemia and the reduction of cardiovascular risk. By belonging to the same ATC category, inclisiran and evolocumab share a therapeutic purpose of lowering lipid levels, although they may differ in their specific mechanisms of action and dosing regimens. Recognizing them as part of the “Other lipid modifying agents” group helps clinicians understand their role within the broader spectrum of lipid‑lowering therapies and supports informed decision‑making in patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 880
content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, classified as plain lipid‑modifying drugs, comprise a group of statin medications that share the same mechanism of action—blocking the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme to reduce cholesterol synthesis. The specific agents that belong to this class are fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin. By lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and modestly raising high‑density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol, these statins are central to the management of hyperlipidaemia and the prevention of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Understanding that fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin are all members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor class helps clinicians select appropriate lipid‑lowering therapy and anticipate class‑wide effects and potential adverse reactions.

As plain lipid‑modifying drugs, these statins are used to manage hyperlipidaemia and reduce cardiovascular risk in patients with elevated cholesterol. Recognizing that fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin share this mechanism of action and therapeutic category helps clinicians select appropriate statin therapy based on individual drug properties, efficacy, and safety profiles.

This drug class works by inhibiting the HMG CoA reductase enzyme, the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and decreased risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. As plain lipid‑modifying agents, these statins are commonly prescribed for the treatment of hyperlipidaemia and for primary and secondary prevention of cardiovascular events. title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 948
content: The category “Other lipid modifying agents in ATC” comprises a diverse group of drugs that are used to influence lipid metabolism. Included in this class are the small‑molecule agents inclisiran, ezetimib, bempedoic acid, lomitapide, as well as the monoclonal antibodies alirocumab and evolocumab. Natural compounds policosanol and tiadenol are also members of this group, and both are identified as fatty alcohols (mastné alkoholy). Among the agents, ezetimib and bempedoic acid are specifically indicated for the treatment of type II hyperlipidemia (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني). This collection of lipid‑modifying therapies—ranging from novel RNA‑targeting drugs and PCSK9 inhibitors to traditional fatty‑alcohol supplements—illustrates the multiple pharmacologic strategies available for managing elevated lipid levels and reducing cardiovascular risk.

Their classification as plain lipid‑modifying drugs underscores their primary therapeutic role in managing dyslipidaemia without additional pharmacologic actions.

content: Atorvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the therapeutic class known as HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This class of agents works by inhibiting the enzyme HMG‑CoA reductase, a key step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, thereby lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and overall plasma lipid levels. As plain lipid‑modifying drugs, these statins are commonly prescribed to reduce cardiovascular risk in patients with hypercholesterolemia, atherosclerotic disease, or other dyslipidemias, and they form a cornerstone of lipid‑lowering therapy in contemporary medical practice.

title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 34
content: Mipomersen, a lipid‑modifying agent, exerts a physiologic effect of decreasing protein synthesis and is classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “Other lipid modifying agents.” This ATC class also includes inclisiran, ezetimibe, bempedoic acid, evolocumab, lomitapide, policosanol and tiadenol. Both ezetimibe and bempedoic acid are indicated for the treatment of type II hyperlipidemia, highlighting the therapeutic focus of several members of this group on abnormal lipid metabolism. Policosanol and tiadenol are chemically related to fatty alcohols, reflecting a shared structural category within the broader lipid‑modifying spectrum. In a separate pharmacologic category, argatroban and dabigatran etexilate belong to the class of direct thrombin inhibitors, agents that target thrombin to prevent clot formation. The heterocyclic compound benzimidazole functions as an inhibitor of both dabigatran etexilate and lomitapide, indicating a potential interaction or modulatory role across distinct therapeutic classes—anticoagulation and lipid modification. Overall, the knowledge base links specific drugs to their mechanistic classes, therapeutic indications, and chemical relationships, providing a comprehensive view of how lipid‑lowering agents, anticoagulants, and related chemical entities intersect in clinical pharmacology and patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 224
content: The cardiovascular pharmacotherapy domain includes lipid‑modifying agents, which are divided into a plain subgroup and other subgroups. Within the plain subgroup, Nicotinic acid and derivatives, lipid‑modifying, plain belong to the chemical family nicofuranose and are also linked to peripheral vasodilators; the broader category Nicotinic acid and derivatives encompasses these plain agents. Another plain subgroup, designated as Other lipid‑modifying agents in ATC, comprises inclisiran, ezetimib, bempedoic acid, evolocumab, and lomitapide. This subgroup is associated with compounds such as policosanol and tiadenol, the latter mapping to fatty alcohols (mastné alkoholy). Both ezetimib and bempedoic acid are indicated for type II hyperlipidemia. Lomitapide, a benzimidazole‑based microsomal triglyceride transfer protein inhibitor, is used for severe hyperlipidaemia, illustrating the therapeutic use of the heterocyclic benzimidazole scaffold. Policosanol, a mixture of long‑chain aliphatic fatty alcohols derived from plant waxes, is employed as a dietary supplement to support cholesterol management. In addition to lipid‑modifying therapy, the cardiovascular drug class includes direct thrombin inhibitors, namely argatroban and dabigatran etexilát, the latter being chemically a benzimidazole; direct thrombin inhibitors provide anticoagulant effects. Overall, the hierarchy demonstrates how lipid‑modifying agents—including nicotinic acid derivatives, inclisiran, ezetimib, bempedoic acid, evolocumab, and lomitapide—are organized within cardiovascular treatment, how specific chemical families such as nicofuranose, benzimidazole, and fatty alcohols are leveraged to regulate lipid metabolism, and how direct thrombin inhibitors contribute to anticoagulation.

title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 65
content: The pharmacologic hierarchy places nicotinic acid and its derivatives as a broad class, within which the subclass nicotinic acid and derivatives, lipid‑modifying, plain (a type of lipid‑modifying agents, plain) is chemically linked to the nicofuranose family and includes peripheral‑vasodilator forms that exert vasodilatory effects. Another ATC subclass, “Other lipid‑modifying agents in ATC,” comprises mipomersen, inclisiran, ezetimib, bempedoic acid, evolocumab, lomitapide, policosanol and tiadenol; mipomersen lowers lipids by decreasing protein synthesis, ezetimib and bempedoic acid are indicated for type II hyperlipidemia (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني) with bempedoic acid also described as a hypoglycemic agent, and policosanol and tiadenol are structurally related to fatty alcohols (mastné alkoholy). Direct thrombin inhibitors, represented by argatroban and dabigatran etexilát, act by directly inhibiting thrombin; dabigatran etexilát is further associated with the benzimidazole scaffold, which functions as an ISA inhibitor of both dabigatran etexilát and lomitapide. Flibanserin, chemically related to the benzimidazole scaffold, acts as a P‑glycoprotein inhibitor and its use is contraindicated with cytochrome P450 3A4 inducers such as klobazam due to enzyme‑mediated interactions. These interconnections illustrate how lipid‑modifying agents, hypoglycemic compounds, anticoagulants, enzyme inducers, and transport inhibitors are organized within pharmacologic classifications and how their mechanisms intersect, guiding drug selection and safety considerations in the management of dyslipidemia, cardiovascular disease, and related metabolic disorders.

title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2741
content: Fibrates, lipid modifying drugs, plain constitute a class of lipid‑modifying agents that are used to improve abnormal blood lipid profiles, particularly by lowering triglyceride levels and modestly raising high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol. The primary members of this plain fibrate class are bezafibrát, klofibrát, and fenofibrát. All three agents act as agonists of peroxisome proliferator‑activated receptor‑α (PPAR‑α), leading to increased oxidation of fatty acids, reduced hepatic production of very‑low‑density lipoprotein, and enhanced clearance of triglyceride‑rich lipoproteins. Because of these mechanisms, bezafibrát, klofibrát, and fenofibrát are employed in the management of hypertriglyceridaemia, mixed dyslipidaemia, and certain cases of secondary dyslipidaemia, contributing to cardiovascular risk reduction when used appropriately in patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2772
content: Bile acid sequestrants are a class of lipid‑lowering agents that function by binding bile acids in the intestine, thereby reducing cholesterol reabsorption and prompting the liver to convert more cholesterol into bile acids. Two commonly used members of this class are colestipol and colesevelam, both of which share the same mechanism of action and are employed to help manage hypercholesterolemia, particularly when statin therapy alone is insufficient or contraindicated. By decreasing low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels, these agents contribute to cardiovascular risk reduction and are an important therapeutic option in comprehensive dyslipidemia management.

Within this group, mipomersen is noted to produce a physiologic effect of decreased protein synthesis. Both ezetimib and bempedoic acid are indicated as potential treatments for type II hyperlipidemia, highlighting their therapeutic role in managing elevated lipid levels. Policosanol and tiadenol are further identified as fatty alcohols (“mastné alkoholy”), linking them chemically to the class of lipid‑modifying agents. Collectively, these agents represent a diverse set of pharmacologic options that target lipid metabolism through various mechanisms, ranging from inhibition of cholesterol absorption (ezetimib) and inhibition of ATP‑citrate lyase (bempedoic acid) to antisense inhibition of apolipoprotein B synthesis (mipomersen) and monoclonal antibody‑mediated PCSK9 inhibition (alirocumab, evolocumab). title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 473
content: Mipomersen, inclisiran, evolocumab, policosanol, and tiadenol are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “Other lipid modifying agents.” Mipomersen exerts its lipid‑lowering effect by decreasing protein synthesis, specifically reducing apolipoprotein B production. Inclisiran and evolocumab also belong to this class but act through distinct mechanisms: inclisiran is a small interfering RNA that silences PCSK9 expression, while evolocumab is a monoclonal antibody that inhibits PCSK9 activity. Policosanol and tiadenol are fatty‑alcohol compounds, as indicated by their mapping to the term “mastné alkoholy” (fatty alcohols). Together, these agents illustrate the diversity of therapeutic strategies encompassed by the “Other lipid modifying agents” category, ranging from antisense oligonucleotides and RNA‑based therapies to monoclonal antibodies and fatty‑alcohol supplements, all aimed at managing dyslipidemia.
